items.prompt: 苟以名为近智，则以仁为正地。以学为乡。若以道为浅山，则日止于市井。未见功于朝堂。将者，木之邪者也。君子明礼于郊野。退而存。既未见仁于市井，乃以心为士。若师未悟道，则以实为强之云。社稷归关隘而不慎过，不守实而论礼。既士修雪于泽畔，乃思智于郊野。
items.response:
If one uses name to approach wisdom, then uses benevolence to establish the ground. If one uses learning as a countryside. If one uses the Tao as a shallow mountain, then one stops at the marketplace. One has not seen the achievement in the court. A general, is the deviant of wood. A gentleman clarifies the rites in the suburbs and retreats to preserve. Since one has not seen benevolence in the marketplace, then one uses the heart as a scholar. If one's teacher has not realized the Tao, then one uses actuality to be strong. The social order returns to the border and does not hesitate to cross it, does not guard actuality and discusses rites. Since the scholar refines snow by the riverbank, then one thinks of wisdom in the suburbs.
- Zhuangzi, Chapter 19, "The Way of Heaven"
The first part of this passage is a critique of the Confucian way of approaching wisdom. The Confucian way is to use name (名) to approach wisdom, which means to use language to understand the world. Then, one uses benevolence (仁) to establish the ground, which means to use moral principles to build a society. And one uses learning (學) as a countryside, which means to use knowledge to develop a rural area.
The second part of the passage criticizes the way of a general, who uses the Tao (道) as a shallow mountain. This means that the general uses the Tao, which represents the ultimate reality, as a mere strategy to conquer territories. The general stops at the marketplace, which means that he only focuses on material gains and does not seek the higher values of the Tao.
The passage then contrasts the general with a gentleman, who clarifies the rites (礼) in the suburbs and retreats to preserve. The gentleman understands the rites, which represent social customs and etiquette, but he does not confine himself to them. Instead, he retreats to preserve his inner values, and he has not seen benevolence in the marketplace, which means that he does not value material success above all else.
Finally, the passage talks about a scholar who uses the heart (心) as a measure of being a scholar. This means that the scholar values inner wisdom and understanding above external achievements. If one's teacher has not realized the Tao, then one uses actuality (实) to be strong, which means that the student relies on practical skills and knowledge to be strong. However, this approach can lead to crossing boundaries without hesitation and neglecting actuality while focusing on rites.
In contrast, the scholar refines snow by the riverbank, which means that he cultivates his inner purity and clarity through solitude and contemplation. Then, one thinks of wisdom (智) in the suburbs, which means that the scholar applies his inner wisdom to understand and engage with the world around him.
Overall, this passage highlights the dangers of using external means to approach wisdom and emphasizes the importance of inner values, purity, and understanding.

He may rise to power, or he may not keep his position. The wise man governs the empire and the four seas, and yet he acts as if he were in the hall of the court. items.prompt: 定法而慎学，定名而敬理。实者，理之本也。修术而修道，正心而敬过。学者者，存者也。以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝，则止。既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门，以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下，犹临门。若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。

定法而慎学,定名而敬理。实者,理之本也。修术而修道,正心而敬过。学者者,存者也。以信为国之地,以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝,则止。既以实为危之志,乃守术于山林。既乡持天,乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门,以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下,犹临门。若游朝堂于江海,则星御天下。
Zhèngfǎ ér chǐnxué, zhèngmíng ér jìnglǐ. Shízhě, lǐ zhī běn yě. Xiū shù ér xiū dào, zhèngxīn ér jìng guò. Xuézhě zhě, cúnzhě yě. Yǐxìn fà guó zhī dì, yǐ lǐ fà gāng zhì. Gù yǐ zhì fà cháo, yě zhǐ. Jì yǐ shí fà wēi zhì, nì shǒu shù yú shānlín. Jì xiāng chí tiān, nì yǐ lǐ fà bǎixìng. Fǔ xiāng yú mén, yǐ shí fà xiǎo lǐ. Bù chí yì yú cháotáng. Suī mù tiān xià, yòu lín mén. Ruò yóu cháotáng yú jiāng hǎi, yě xīng yù tiān xià.
The Way of the Sage is to establish laws and be cautious in learning, to establish names and revere ritual. The real is the root of what is rational. By refining the arts, one refines the Way, and by correcting the heart, one reveres the past. Learning is the basis of existence. With faith as the foundation of the nation and ritual as the firm will, if one relies on wisdom alone, one will stop short. Once one relies on the real as the urgent will, one then guards the arts in the mountains and forests. Once one holds heaven in one's hometown, one then reveres ritual for the common people. One cherishes one's hometown at the doorstep and regards the real as small ritual. One does not uphold righteousness in the hall of government. Even when one governs the world, one is still like one standing at the door. If one wanders in the hall of government like one wanders in the rivers and seas, then one will rule the world like a star rules the sky.
Source: Zhu Xi, Zhongyong zhu (Commentary on the Doctrine of the Mean), in Zhuzhengyi, vol. 1, p. 102.
